Treas Sec Shultz, at briefing on Nixon Adm's '75 budget, says 1 way to counteract recession is for Fed Reserve to follow 'classic' expansionist policy in supplying credit needs (S)

Africa, Her Hopes Dashed, Faces Uncertainty
survey on African econ outlook says higher world oil prices threaten recent econ gain; notes that in addition to higher oil costs, Africa will face increased prices for imports from indus nations seeking to offset own increased oil bills (Internatl Econ Survey) (M)
‘INFLATION IS SEEN IN COMMODITIES

‘Sluggish Growth’ Is Seen For Economy Next Year
Forum of Conference Bd on Nov 13 predicts that severe cutback in energy supplies will pose substantial threat to business conditions in '74; forum sees '74 as yr of discontent, uncertainty and sluggish growth resulting from energy crisis

BUSINESS ROUND‐UP
bond specialists are pondering impact that higher gasoline prices and gas shortages might have on toll rds, which have been built by floating huge bond issues; L F Rothschild & Co analyst D H Stevens says that modest reduction in motor vehicle traffic will make it difficult for some tpk auths to maintain interest payments on debt; cites latest rept of ailing Chesapeake Bay Bridge and Tunnel Dist that points up danger; illus

Home Builders’ Very Own Recession
Housing indus is having recession and housing starts in US were at annual rate of 1.36‐million in Dec ‘73, down from 1.7‐million in Nov ‘73 and from avg monthly rate of 2.4‐million in 1st qr of ‘73; repercussions are being felt in appliances, lumber, carpeting, furniture and construction employment; home sales dropped drastically in last half of ‘73 when interest on mortgages climbed to 9% or more in some areas; construction materials have also climbed in price and land values have gone up; in addition some bldg materials are in short supply; builders across US comment; George C Martin, pres of Govt Natl Mortgage Assn, says Govt is slow to realize that nation is heading toward housing crisis; illus; graphs showing number of housing starts from Dec ‘72 through Dec ‘73 and rise in mortgage rates from Dec ‘72 through Dec ‘73 (L)

Oil and the Economy
L Silk says every Amer corp wants to know how energy crisis will affect it; says econ forecasting team headed by F P Murphy is making study for GE, using input-output model based on work of Prof W W Leontief; study's predictions of impact on US econ of Arab oil embargo are based on certain assumptions: that Arab state will yield to intense pressure and resume oil shipments to US by June '74, that curtailment of oil shipments to Japan and Western Eur will not be escalated further and may be eased and that Fed spending will increase enough to offset fiscal impact of any tax increases that may arise from fuel shortages; concludes that energy crisis is serious but manageable; estimates shortfall of 2.1 million bbls daily for 1st qr '74, about 11% less than normal demand of 19.5 million bbls; forecasts more extreme shortages in fuel oils (boiler fuel, diesel fuel and heating oil), which it says will fall 21% short of demand; estimates that mfg sector of econ as whole will suffer deficit of 14% of its normal fuel oil requirements in 1st qr '74, with larger shortfall in New England, 30%, and Middle Atlantic states, 20%; say lack of rail cars, barges, ships, storage tanks and unloading facilities will limit transshipments of any oil surpluses that may become available; cannot say how much fuel nation's mfrs have hoarded and can use to keep plants operating if oil supplies run lower than expected; on basis of relatively hopeful assumptions about mid-'74 end of Arab oil embargo, GE economists expect impact of energy crisis on production to be steep, short and highly selective, with heaviest damage to consumer outlays and indus spending, especially on cars, trucks and transportation-related services; say that if embargo were extended throughout '74, econ would go into deep recession, with GNP falling in all 4 qrs and unemployment rate climbing to 8% in 3d qr and to 8.5% in 4th; but think fear of backlash from Western countries will prevent Arabs from extending oil cuts and embargo that long

Good‐by to Controls ...
Ed on Nixon Adm lifting of wage-price controls holds that only bright spot in anti-inflation picture has been continence of organized labor in face of sharp ascent in living costs; sees 3% decrease in purchasing power spurring rank-and-file demands in '74 for bigger wage boosts or for universal adoption of escalator clauses

The Nation
rev of week's events in Mitchell-Stans trial; Mitchell illus (M)

Impact of Embargo Lingers On
article on longrange impact of Arab oil policies on US and world econ (M)

European Scene
comment on econ dislocations in W Eur and threatened recession from Arab oil embargo

G. E. Reports Profit Records
GE chmn Reginald H Jones on Jan 24 expects earnings will reach $585‐million, compared with $530‐million in ‘72, news conf; repts 4th qr income of $191‐million, compared with ‘72 4th qr profits of $177.2‐million; illus (M)

Black Mayors and Rights Chiefs Find a Sympathetic Ear in Ford
Nixon aide Stanley A Scott repts impact of oil shortage on natl econ was primary topic at Jan 11 meeting between group of 16 black majors and Vice Pres Ford that was arranged by Scott at Ford's behest (S)

No Real Harm to U.S. Seen in Oil Cuts
OECD predicts on Dec 20 that Arab oil embargo will not seriously impair Amer indus production in '74 because Arab sources fill only 6% of Amer energy consumption and US use of oil for nonproductive purposes is extremely high and could be cut by as much as 15% without significantly affecting productive capacity

Economists Plead Uncertainty on 1974 Outlook
Comment on forecasts for '74 finds economists wary because of their blunders in '73, the energy shortage, and uncertainty about the future; sees mild recession in 1st half of '74, with slight drop in GNP; sees econ activity speeding up in 2d half, with worse inflation and slight drop in profits; former chmn of Econ Advisers Council A M Okun says '73 error was 'one of the greatest failures of econ analysis in modern times'some economists feel they have over-emphasized macro-economics and neglected micro-economics, the study of industries and cos; this yr they are studying fuel

Business Briefs
Eur aircraft mfrs on Oct 23 ask EEC for urgent help in their struggle to obtain more business; reptdly want community to adopt Eur aeronautical policy fully supported by member govts so that indus can become more competitive

Beanie's ‘In House’ Work Policy Is Debated
Mayor Beame adm promise to have architectural and engineering work done by dept 'in-house' tech personnel, in effort to strengthen Civil Service, seen as almost certain to increase Civil Service ranks at time of extreme budget-consciousness (S)

Two Surveys Cautious About Economy on L.I.
Recent LI Assn of Commerce and Indus and Franklin Natl Bank surveys show that energy crisis is expected to lower profits of LI business in ‘74 but econ outlook for Nassau and Suffolk (NY) Counties is cautiously optimistic; surveys’ results outlined; assn exec dir Fred E Merrill comments

People and Business
PVO Internatl exec Frederic W Hammersfahr says one of most important factors in expanding US trade with USSR and China will be licensing of tech, speech, Licensing Execs Soc, NYC; notes licensing has potential for letting one enter foreign mkts and can become basis for highly profitable joint ventures (S)

Paper Shortage Plagues Business
paper shortage that has plagued US business in ‘73 is expected to continue for some time; situation is so extreme that US Paperworkers Internatl Union in Dec bought ad in major newspapers to appeal to Govt to let pulp and paper prices be determined by mkt forces and not by Govt controls; Amer Paper inst pres E A Locke Jr says assuming that econ activity continues at its current pace, he does not see situation improving; Internatl paper Co execs have suggested that return on investment of 8% to 10%, rather than going rate of 5% before interest, would be needed to encourage newsprint makers to plunge into new ventures; Locke repts that indus spent $98‐million on primary facilities during ‘73 but that $480‐million of total was expended on pollution control equipment (Natl Econ Survey) (M)

General Tax Cut Opposed by Burns; Longer Executive‐Pay Curbs Asked
Fed Reserve Chmn Burns tells HR Appropriations com that easier Fed Reserve money and credit policy, appropriate for typical recession, would not help current problem because of unusual factor of oil shortage; his illus (S)

Sudden Spanish Crackdown Shakes Thin Hopes for Some Liberalization
article discusses recent pol crisis in Spain resulting from energy shortage, execution of Catalan anarchist Salvador Puig Antich, despite thousands of clemency pleas, and police detention of Vizcaya Prov Bishop Antonio Anoveros Ataun, who called for greater freedom for Basques; events prompted occasional rioting by Barcelona and Madrid Univ students; other incidents contributing to unrest include '72 pol case in which 113 Govt oppositionists were arrested for holding 'illegal assembly' in Barcelona ch bldg and '72 refusal by Justice Min to allow 2 candidates for Madrid Bar Assn pres to be listed on ballots; events seen as reasons for skepticism about new Premier Carlos Arias Navarro's promised pol changes delivered in Feb 12 speech describing elective system of mayors and prov assembly heads, new pol and labor assns and clearer separation of Parliament from Govt; Govt's sudden show of toughness toward dissident groups also contributes to skepticism; pol attitudes in Madrid compared to those already prevalent in Barcelona; map (L)

Trudeau Says Canadians Won't Have Oil Shortage
Canadian Prime Min Trudeau on Dec 30 says that Canadians will not experience serious oil shortages and doubts energy crisis will lead to econ slowdown in '74

Is Budget Deficit Big Enough?
Charles L Schultze article on changes in econ as factor behind differences in Pres Nixon's fiscal '74 and fiscal '75 budgets; notes options Pres had in drawing up '75 budget and reasons for course he chose; holds there will be actual budget deficit of $9-billion; cartoon

‘Judgmental Factors’ of a Recession
L Silk analysis of issue facing econ policy-makers in '74 of whether to deploy their forces against recession or inflation, both of which are being exacerbated by energy shortage; says issue has become somewhat confused by Nixon's assertion 'there will be no recession'; examines question of what constitutes a recession and quotes Treas Sec George P Shultz on that and related matters; also examines question whether Nixon, if he 'busts' budget to avoid recession, would simply worsen inflation (M)

People and Business
Israeli Econ Min A Sher on Nov 21 predicts that there will be rise in foreign investment in Israel, int; says that Middle East peace settlement will widen export opportunities in Middle East; says Israel's econ is rapidly recovering from recent war
10‐POINT PROPOSAL

Brimmer of Federal Reserve Finds Black Job Gains in 1973
Fed Reserve System member Andrew F Brimmer, in rept, says that blacks in '73 made marked gains in participation in labor force, over-all employment and expanded opportunities in higher-paying blue-collar and professional jobs; says there was moderate increase in black unemployment; says unemployment rate for blacks increased by 1.2% since recent low in Oct, to 9.6% in Jan; says employment outlook in '74 for black workers is pessimistic because of econ slowdown and energy crisis; recommends higher funding of public employment programs, extension of unemployment ins benefits and special training programs that are under consideration by Cong to ease impact on black workers; repts increase of black participation in labor force was most prominent among women and young workers; there were 10-million blacks looking for or holding jobs in '73, up 330,000 from '72; jobs held by blacks increased by 400,000 to 9.3-million (M)

Oil‐Supply Situation Spurs Suggestion
former Asst Sec R N Gardner on Nov 14 urges carefully drawn amendments to current trade bill to put oil-exporters on notice they cannot wage econ war on US with impunity; illus

Numismatics
Article holds that US Bur of the Mint is studying problem of a substitute for increasingly expensive copper that makes up 95% of 1c coin, and is also launching long-range probe into how many coins of all denominations are likely to be needed in everyday commerce 2 yrs, or even 20 yrs, from now; inauguration of 2 studies was recently reptd by Mint dir M T Brooks through Treas Dept; Brooks, apprearing as guest speaker at luncheon meeting of Natl Automatic Merchandising Assn, Washington, DC, last mo, urged officials of vending machine indus to conduct surveys of their business outlook for purpose of determining future coin usage requirements; asserted that information will be of vital importance in helping Bur to more accurately meet future coin demands; cited meeting of new Bicentennial coinage law that calls for Amer Revolution commemorative designs; discussed Mint's plans for new branch in Denver to be completed in '80; reptd that Coinage Study Group has been set up in Treas Dept, and 1 assignment is to study fluctuating prices in metal mkts which may necessitate substitution of less costly materials--not necessarily metallic--in 1 or more coins

Zero Traffic Growth Threatens Airlines

Prices of Stocks Plummet; Dow Industrials Sink 24.24
energy crisis and Arab oil export embargo cited as factors in 24.24 drop in Dow Jones indus avg

Nixon Sounds a Warning On Raw‐Material Prices
Pres Nixon's 2d annual internatl econ rept contains discussion on world food problems; finds there is considerable disagreement as to whether current food gap is permanent; states that most agr experts believe that current situation will continue in foreseeable future (S)

‘Do Something!’
W Safire article on Nixon's pronouncement 'there will be no recession' and on concern by his aides Treas Sec George P Shultz and Econ Advisers Council chmn Herbert Stein on Nixon's decision for pol reasons to 'do something'; recalls Shultz confrontation with Sen William Proxmire over need for 'action'; questions wisdom of doing almost anything to avoid a recession, citing effects on inflation

14% Rise in Capital Outlays Is Expected
McGraw-Hill survey to be released Nov 9 shows indus plans to spend record $113.9-billion in '74 for new plant and equipment, 14% above estimated spending in '73; results of survey do not take into acct uncertain effects of oil shortage and energy allocations; survey, made in Oct, shows 2/3 of businessmen believe real GNP will grow 1 to 3.4% in '74, 30% see normal growth of 3.5% or more, only 3% see recession; 25 of 26 major industries expect to increase dollar sales, 3 of 26 see drop in sales; D Greenwald, chief economist for McGraw-Hill, says many of those surveyed believe price controls will end early in '74; says capital spending should be source of strength in econ outlook despite effects of inflation barring drastic impact of energy restrictions; data on projected spending in '74 in various indus sectors; Greenwald says 14% rise in spending represents spillover of '73 spending into '74 because of shortages on supply side, lack of materials and skilled labor

ENERGY MEN FIND NIXON PLAN WEAK
T C Gaines, chief economist of Mfrs Hanover Trust Co, predicts real growth rate of near zero in '74, partially because of energy shortage; says Pres Nixon's energy program will do little to improve this gen econ outlook; L H Olsen, chief economist of First Natl City Bank, warns against too much pessimism about the economy due to energy shortage; says no one knows what the impact of shortage will be; says some of the figures presented have not been fully thought out; repr of Continental Illinois Natl Bank & Trust Co says says energy shortages will not be severe, and there will be no recession given reasonable public policies and some voluntary private restraint

THE ECONOMIC SCENE
tough approach is advocated by those who think energy emergency will be lengthy; official Govt stance is that crisis will be transitory; crisis has begun to make impact on certain industries although full effects will not be felt until winter weather comes; economists responses to queries on how to handle crisis vary

THE ECONOMIC SCENE
Comment on econ outlook and inflation; growing sense of unease, tension and discontent over state of econ seen hardly warranted by over-all performance and prospects, but grave concern over pattern of price indexes is seen justified; inflation called Public Enemy No. 1

Nominee as Adviser Emphasizes Need for Consistency
Prof W J Fellner says controlling inflation requires consistent Govt policy of forcing slowdowns in economy whenever inflation rate exceeds pre-established acceptable level, speech Oct 10 at Fed Statistics Users conf, Washington; stresses need for Govt follow-through on slowdown threats to establish credibility; says credibility engenders voluntary restraint; former chmn of Econ Advisers Council A M Okun, also speaking at conf, optimistic on future price trends; says '73 inflation was not caused by errors in Govt policies but by supply and demand imbalances; Fellner illus

ECONOMIC OUTLOOK HELD POOR IN STATE

Point of View
Albert H Cox Jr comment on econ policies says traditional fiscal and monetary policies are unconnected with Arab oil embargo; says econ policy makers will have to continue their study of US energy problems to insure reasonable growth because Arabs probably will not provide sufficient energy after embargo is lifted; says Fed tax benefits for energy conservation and penalties for wastage are probable; asserts cumulative effect of US anti‐pollution laws may acct for entire energy shortfall and must be evaluated in that light; asserts price US will pay for cleaner environment will be in jobs (Natl Econ Survey) (M)

Fed May Be Sharply Expanding Credit Supply, Analysts Assert
credit mkt analysts believe Fed Reserve may have initiated vigorous expansion of credit supply, spurred by energy shortage that has made an econ downturn more likely in 74; latest Reserve data do not reflect any massive stimulation of econ but they are entirely consistent with credit expansion; money supply shows avg annual rate of gain of 6.5% for latest 2 mos, well ahead of growth rate of 2.5% for most recent 4 mos and higher than 5.5% figure in latest 12; Reserve figures also reveal that short-term interest rates were somewhat easier in period ended Nov 28, that amt of outstanding commercial paper rose by $1.7-billion in wk ended Nov 21, that business loans at major NYC banks rose by $84-million to 2-wk total of $198-million; other figures

POINT OF VIEW
W W Ebanks article discusses flaws in Employment Act of '71, which provides Govt jobs; discusses relationship between Govt's 'involvement' in providing jobs in public sector and its effect on private sector; holds Nixon Adm view is that it is all right for state and local govts to increase employment with Fed money as long as Fed employment does not expand; drawing

Britain on a Tightrope
ed, commenting on Brit Labor Govt's 1st budget, holds that it is ‘neutral pkg,’ aimed at reassuring domestic and foreign forces on which econ heavily depends while making some gestures toward Govt supporters and programs advocated in Feb; cites several Labor party programs passed, including removal from tax roll of 1.5‐million Britons at bottom of econ ladder, increasing taxes on corporate profits and higher personal income taxes; sees irony in Labor Govt being constrained to cut deficit spending by some $3.5‐billion and having to float large loan; hails Chancellor of the Exchequer Denis Healey's ability to handle situation

THE ECONOMIC SCENE
Article discusses rising militancy of labor wage negotiations and threat of increased strikes in '74; holds that relatively quiet and agreeable yr on labor front is turning into rather tense one in waning mos of '73, posing worrisome threat for '74, when large number of contract renewals come up in several key industries

THE ECONOMIC SCENE
comment on '73 as yr of crisis notes present concern in business world about energy crisis; cites Nixon's call for conservation, need for developing untapped sources and expediting expansion of atomic energy

MBFR Deadlock
ed on resumption of Vienna conf on mutual reduction of forces in Central Eur supports stiffening of NATO resistance to Soviet proposals that maintain and could even increase Warsaw Pact's present preponderance of power; holds key word is 'balance' if mil situation in Central Eur is to be stabilized

NJ Labor and Indus Comr R M Heymann, asserting on Oct 13 that NJ will go through 'mild econ slowdown' in next 2 yrs, notes that level of unemployment should remain essentially unchanged at present rate of 7% during econ readjustment
LESSER CALENDAR OF FINANCING SET

United Artists Pictures Corp repts film Papillon has grossed more than $18-million

G. E. Bomber Role Protested by Marchers

corporate bond dealers on Dec 5, commenting on mkt decline, say losses reflect econ uncertainty in face of energy problems

Agr expert J H McManigal recently declared that over past 5 yrs farmland has proved to be better investment than stock or bonds, noting that recent surge in land prices has been stimulated mainly by extraordinary gains in farm income; net farm income is forecast as record $24-billion in '73, 20% higher than '72; illus

Who's running this thing? Nixonomics

BURLINGTON HEAD MAY LEAVE EARLY
Charles F Myers jr, chmn of Burlington Industries Inc, tells stockholders he is considering early retirement but would remain dir of co; Horace C Jones, pres and chief exec officer, is expected to become chmn; Jones tells meeting that Burlington is optimistic about '74 business outlook; co reptd 12% gain in sales and 48% rise in earnings for 1st fiscal qr ended Dec 29, '73 (M)

Year of Twin Crises
econ analysis of factors influencing NY stock mkts holds uncertainty about impact of US shortage and Arab embargo on business imparts uncertain feeling among securities purchasers

Light Amid the Gloom
comment on econ outlook in light of energy crisis finds reasons for optimism despite seriousness of problems; suggests possibility that problems are being overexaggerated and that ingenuity in coping with them is being underestimated; notes surprising cooperation of business and pub in conserving energy, alacrity of Washington in dealing with energy crisis, and a better understanding of economy's problem, in business and Govt, with passage of time; cites views expressed by former chmn of Econ Advisers Council P W McCracken in an article last wk on relationship between econ growth and rise in energy consumption; McCracken says there has been an increase in productivity of energy, and that on the avg, there is more than 1% increase in output for each percentage point increase in energy use; notes wide variation in relationship of energy used and GNP from yr to yr; in '70, GNP declined 0.4% with 5.6% increase in energy used; in '71, GNP rose 3.2% with 0.4% increase in energy used

THE ECONOMIC SCENE
article discusses private and Govt economists' views on econ outlook for '74 in light of energy shortage; suggests that economists may be unduly minimizing possible econ consequences of harsh brandishment of oil weapon by Arab nations since Eur and Japan may be severely affected by Arab cutbacks, thereby exerting considerable influence on Amer econ; notes public concern over energy situation has been reflected most acutely in steep decline in stock mkt and Adm concern is being reflected in series of moves toward allocation and conservation programs and efforts to start development of alternative sources

Real G.N.P. Growth Was3.6% in Quarter
Commerce Dept repts 3d qr real growth in GNP was 3.6%; actual annual rate, without eliminating effects of inflation, was $1,304-billion, a growth of $32-billion or 10.4%; real growth rate compares with smaller 2.4% rate in 2d qr; inflation rate in GNP is at annual rate of 6.7% in 3d qr compared with 7.3% in 2d qr; chart showing GNP and inflation, '63 to 3d qr of '73; Asst Sec of Commerce S L Jones welcomes slowdown and says he would be happy with a growth rate of some 3.4% in 4th qr; says there still are strong inflationary pressures which he believes will continue; Fed Reserve Bd repts mfg plants operated at 83.4% of capacity in the 3d qr, slightly above 83.3% in 2d qr; repts index of mfg output rose to 126.5 from 124.8; repts indus capacity index rose to 151.6 from 149.8

Nigerians, Rejecting Rural Lives, Flock to Capital
current scene, Lagos (Nigeria), described; some urban problems outlined; illus (L)

New Housing Starts Fall; Personal Income Higher
Former chmn of Econ Advisers Council P W McCracken, M K Evans of Chase Econometric Assocs and P A Samuelson of MIT testify at hearings by Cong Joint Econ Com in its periodic reassessment of econ; McCracken says econ outlook is better than indicated by sour attitude of much of public, but that prospects for continued inflation, particularly in indus, may be worse than most people think; sees '74 business investment limited more by ability of capital-goods industries to deliver than by demand; sees unlikely any large shift to inventory liquidation; says inventories would have to go up some $20-billion to approximate conditions of past downturns; Evans says there is virtually no chance that '74 slowdown will not develop on schedule; Samuelson voices fear that Adm will be stampeded by food-price inflation to adopt drastically restrictive fiscal and monetary policies that could usher in serious period of stagnation

The Crucial Capital Spending Boom
capital expenditures for petroleum refining capacity expected to rise; recently announced plans for new facilities still will not provide adequate capacity; US econ seen capable of weathering oil shortage if supplies are distributed with reasonable efficiency

NATO at Twenty‐Five ...
ed on outlook for France following Pompidou's death sees more conciliatory attitude toward US and France's EEC partners should Gaullist Jacques Chaban-Delmas or Repub Ind Min Valery Giscard d'Estaing succeed him

THE ECONOMIC SCENE
Comment on econ outlook; virtually all forecasters now see period of small growth in '74, but no recession; many economists warn against complacency because of recent declines in prices; stress need for continuing tight rein on monetary and fiscal policies; majority also back prompt termination of wage-price controls; many share view of Mellon Bank economist N Robertson: 'The longer Phase 4 remains in place, the greater the dangers of distortions, dislocations and shortages, which can only serve to prolong and even intensify the inflationary spiral'; '74 outlook seen as no occasion for gloom; view or former head of Economic Advisers Council P W McCracken cited

HR votes, 339-21, to increase SBA's lending authority by $575-million until June 30 '74; HR Small Business subcom adds stiff provision prohibiting 'improper influence' from forces outside SBA and continues its investigation of allegations ranging from 'possible criminal activities to poor mgt' in 21 SBA field offices; Repr E I Koch argued against passage of bill, saying investigation should be completed and safeguards added before passage; subcom chmn Repr R G Stephens predicts that investigation will necessitate writing of new SBA legis in next 6 mos

oil shortage seen slowing growth of aerospace industries; impact of shortage on aircraft indus noted; illus (Natl Econ Survey) (M)

correction; Feb 12 article described Mrs Alice Roosevelt Longworth as Pres T Roosevelt's only daughter; Pres had 2 daughters: Longworth by his 1st marriage and Mrs Ethel Roosevelt Derby by his 2d (S)

NORTHEAST NOTEBOOK: Lee, Mass.;
1885 School Is Now an Inn
A FORMER Roman Catholic elementary school building in this Berkshire Mountain community has been moved and converted into the Chambery Inn, whose guest suites incorporate the blackboards and chalk trays of the onetime classrooms. 

The frame structure, built in 1885 as St. Mary's School, was the first parochial school in Berkshire County and the first in the United States operated by the Sisters of St. Joseph of Chambery, a French order that later established other American schools.

From 1957 to 1977, after St. Mary's parish built a new elementary school, this town of about 6,000 residents used the 7,500-square-foot building for a public kindergarten. In 1988, when the local parish planned to demolish the building, Joseph M. Toole bought it for $1 

"I didn't have any plans for it but I didn't want to see it torn down," said Mr. Toole, a Lee native who attended kindergarten in the building. 

He had the 300-ton building moved to a nearby half-acre lot on the corner of Main and Elm Streets next to his family's insurance agency.

Early this year, after unsuccessfully trying to market the building for office space, Mr. Toole decided to make it an inn, which he now operates full time. He estimated the combined cost of the move, site work, remodeling and furnishings at $750,000. 

Mr. Toole said he had borne two-thirds of the costs and had borrowed the rest.

The nine-room inn is on a slight slope and Mr. Toole had a new foundation and lower floor built. There are guest rooms and a dining area on the lower floor. The classrooms in the upper two stories were remodeled as guest suites, with partitions added for closets and bathrooms. Gas fireplaces were also installed. 

Blackboards, tin ceilings, fixtures and other structural features have been retained and no walls were removed, said Mr. Toole, who calls himself a "purist."

The inn opened in October, with rates ranging from $60 to $120 in winter and $70 to $175 in summer. Mr. Toole said that his occupancy rate last month was 45 percent but that he was not worried about undertaking the venture in an economic downturn because of the inn's unusual qualities. 

While the town, which straddles exit 2 of the Massachusetts Turnpike, is a Berkshire County tourist destination and has other inns and visitor-oriented businesses, its economy relies more than other towns nearby -- such as Stockbridge -- on industry and local business.

The Chambery Inn is a mile north of exit 2 across the street from a converted train station that was remodeled in the late 1970's into the Sullivan Station restaurant.

Why the Trade Talks Fizzled
So the world trade talks in Brussels were a bust, and the wicked protectionist European Community is widely portrayed as responsible. Once again the Europeans lost the propaganda battle. The world is to be plunged into trade wars and recession because of a European addiction to subsidizing farmers. 

Here is one European's dissenting view. Two things went wrong. First, the timing turned sour. This was nobody's fault. The two previous General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade negotiations, the Kennedy round in the 1960's and the Tokyo round in the 1970's, were concluded when the economic outlook was bright. This time, the storm signals are out across the world. If a rising tide lifts all boats, a falling tide can deposit them on the mud at the bottom of the harbor.

In the Region: Long Island;
In Long Beach, It's 'Let's Make a Deal'
AFTER almost a decade of intense development, major residential building in the city of Long Beach has come to a virtual standstill. "Developers are still putting up one- and two-family houses, but except for that, we're in a holding pattern right now," said City Manager Edwin L. Eaton. 

Development was so frenzied that new zoning to limit density was adopted three years. But now, oceanfront projects that came on the market after sales began to soften are encountering such meager demand that developers are turning to rentals, auctions and other marketing strategies to fill empty units. Others are even building affordable housing on oceanfront sites while waiting for a stronger market.

THE VIEW FROM: THE COUNCIL FOR THE ARTS;
Making Culture a Priority
WHEN the Council for the Arts began 25 years ago, it was a fledgling group of prominent Westchester residents interested in building a Lincoln Center-type institution in the county. Its first grant, from the State Council for the Arts, was for $1,500. Now the council operates on a yearly budget of $2 million, with a staff of 14, and is the largest private nonprofit arts council in the state.

The council will celebrate its 25th year on Saturday with "Reflections," a sold-out gala to be held at Arrowwood in Rye Brook. Its organizers say it will outshine all the previous benefits and, with a goal of $140,000, the event is "the largest ever," said Melinda Bena, one of its chairwomen.

Your Own Account;
When a Small Business Stumbles
FOUR years ago, when Dr. Stephen I. Hudis of Manhattan bought the practice of a dentist who was retiring, he acquired a solid client base, but plenty of business problems. Patients didn't pay when they had the work done, so he was carrying about $70,000 a month in accounts receivable. Then, when the New York economy turned sour after the 1987 stock market crash, people started to put off major dental work, stretch out the time between routine exams and further delay payments. "That has really affected my business," he said.

Nothing he had learned in dental school helped. "They teach you nothing about business," said Dr. Hudis, who has been practicing for 11 years. "I felt overwhelmed." On the recommendation of a friend, he turned to Lynn Garber, a small-business consultant in Yardley, Pa., who urged him to revamp his system so that bills went out sooner and clients were asked to pay as dental work was done. Within months, he was getting paid for business on the books and doing marketing -- such as sending out champagne to patients who recommended several others -- that, he believed, brought in new business.

VIEW; Why College Costs So Much
The setting is a university administration office. The scene is a meeting between the president and a group of parents. The action begins when one vocal father, an executive with an electronics company, demands to know why the cost of attending this private institution has gone up 57 percent over the last 10 years. The president says that it could be worse -- the price of attending a public university has risen 79 percent. Undeterred, the father points out that over the same period the Consumer Price Index has gone up just 27 percent and the median family income a mere 38 percent.

 ''I understand,'' the president answers soothingly. ''But we have made enormous economies recently.'' Then she coughs gently into a tissue. ''Excuse me,'' she demurs, ''but there's a case of Baumol's disease going around.''

EUROPE GERMANY: ECONOMY STALLS The German econ- omy stagnated in the second quarter in yet more proof that Europe has not been immune from the economic slowdown in the United States. The federal statistics office said gross domestic product in the second quarter had grown 0.6 percent from a year earlier, the lowest level in more than four years, from 1.4 percent in the first quarter. However, the office said, investment, particularly in construction, was stagnant and exports declined. Analysts said, however, that one positive number was the 0.9 percent increase in consumer spending.   (Reuters) BRITAIN: PROFIT UP AT ROLLS-ROYCE Helped by increased sales of commercial jet engines and spare parts, Rolls-Royce said its first-half profit rose more than fourfold. Net income increased to £85 million ($123 million), or 5.35 pence a share, compared with £18 million, or 1.15 pence a share, last year. Sales grew 15 percent, to £3 billion ($4.3 billion). Sales of commercial jet engines grew 40 percent, and the number of engines already on planes that require spare parts and servicing is on the rise, the company said. The No. 2 engine maker in the world, Rolls is winning market share at the expense of Pratt & Whitney, a unit of United Technologies, which is the industry's third largest. General Electric remains the market leader.   Suzanne Kapner (NYT) FINLAND: LAYOFFS AT SONERA Finland's largest mobile phone network, Sonera, plans to lay off about 1,000 people, or nearly 9 percent of its work force, to cut costs. JEREMY BRECHER, of West Cornwall and New Haven, is a writer and historian who has spent most of his adult life listening to the stories of working people in Connecticut and sharing them through articles, books and public television documentaries.

"Strike!" published in 1972, has become a classic of American labor history. His other books include "Brass Valley: The Story of Working People's Lives and Struggles in an American Industrial Region,"written with Jerry Lombardi and Jan Stackhouse, and, most recently, "Global Village or Global Pillage: Economic Reconstruction from the Bottom Up," with Tim Costello. Stocks finished mixed yesterday as investors took the opportunity to lock in profits and drew comfort from the Federal Reserve's benign take on inflation. Technology stocks continued to rally, with the Nasdaq composite index posting its eighth consecutive gain. I.M.F. Warns of Imbalance in World Consumption
The United States is likely to experience slower economic growth next year, and its rapidly rising foreign debt is at the heart of dangerous global imbalances, the International Monetary Fund said Wednesday.     The fund said that global economic growth had become too dependent on a handful of countries, led by the United States, that consume far more than they produce. That imbalance, it warned in its semiannual World Economic Outlook, could lead to a wrenching correction.

World Business Briefing | Americas: Brazil: Vehicle Registrations Slow
New-vehicle registrations in Brazil rose at the slowest pace in three months in June, as higher interest rates slowed demand for expensive goods bought on credit. Registrations of new cars and trucks, made in Brazil and elsewhere, rose 14 percent, to 148,491 units, from 130,697 units in June 2004, the association said in a report distributed in São Paulo. Registrations rose 3.9 percent compared with May. ''The numbers are showing that the economic slowdown is not being exaggerated,'' said Alexandre Lintz, a senior Brazil economist for BNP Paribas. ''This is good news for the central bank because it will ease criticism the bank may have exaggerated the dosage.''

Weakening Hurricane Spurs a Rally After Rough Days
Wall Street rallied to finish mostly higher yesterday, as Hurricane Rita lost strength and hopes increased that its effect on Gulf Coast oil refineries would not be as bad as initially feared.      With Rita headed for the Texas coastline, the heart of the nation's oil production, investors had braced for a repeat of the devastation caused by Hurricane Katrina. By yesterday afternoon, however, the storm weakened two levels to a Category 3 hurricane, sending oil prices down more than $2 and easing the concern on Wall Street.
